summary: Julia Evans shares a beginner-friendly introduction to GDB, the C debugger, motivated by her experience debugging her own operating system. She walks through three fundamental GDB operations: setting breakpoints, viewing disassembled code, and examining memory contents. The post uses a minimal C program to demonstrate each concept with concrete examples. She emphasizes that just knowing breakpoints and memory examination got her surprisingly far in real debugging work.
key_points: GDB can be learned incrementally - just three commands (break, disassemble, x) provide significant debugging capability
Setting breakpoints and using 'where' to inspect the call stack are the most basic but essential GDB operations
The 'examine' (x) command lets you inspect raw memory with different format specifiers like strings or individual characters
Compiling with -g flag is necessary to get useful debugging symbols in GDB
money_quotes: Debugging C programs used to scare me a lot.
The main thing I used gdb for when I was debugging my kernel was to examine regions of memory to make sure they were what I thought they were.
I know that gdb does lots of other stuff, but I still don't know it very well and x and break got me pretty far.
key_insight: You don't need to master GDB to get real value from it - just learning breakpoints and memory examination can carry you surprisingly far in debugging C programs.
